notes: Emerging as de-facto technology standard for integrating SOA infrastructure; 60% of large company respondents using/planning shared messaging; three-phase evolution: message bus → service bus → process bus.
SOA adoption survey (286 companies): 76% implementing Web Services calls; 73% implementing app-related services; 60% implementing shared messaging; 58% governance/management.
76% implementing web services calls to existing applications or planning within 12 months; WS-* stack cited as requirement for 'true ESB' by SOA purists like Cape Clear.
Cape Clear et al. argue true ESB must religiously adhere to WS-* stack; products that simply wrap proprietary legacy middleware do not provide same characteristics or benefits.
ESB phase 1 (message bus): data transformation and reliable routing; traditional role of ESB; first wave of ESBs provided reliable messaging.
ESB phase 2 (service bus): where most of the market is in 2005; enables composite applications and event-based processing; real-time message content analysis.

ESB phase 3 (process bus / mediation): complex manipulations, long process execution; handles SOA decomposition data — applications data, logic, web services, XML messages, BP models, business rules, policies, external data sources.
49% currently use or plan within 12 months; enabled by phase 2 (service bus / orchestration) of ESB evolution.
Aberdeen recommends ESB vendors must interact with or inherit Services Registry characteristics; critical for SOA governance and management.
65% of survey respondents currently use or plan XML gateways/adapters within 12 months.
ESB pioneer/SOA-specific; best-of-breed attractiveness for pure SOAs; faster ROI due to well-defined scope.
Listed in ESB pioneer/SOA-specific segment; IBM also present in platform (WebSphere Message Broker) and process-centric (WebSphere Process Server) segments.
Listed in 'Part of a platform' ESB vendor segment; good fit for service-oriented business applications development; inherent latency and cost overhead from monolithic nature.
Listed in 'Part of a platform' ESB vendor segment; composite application enablement; legacy SOA enablement.
Traditional EAI/EII players: IONA, Informatica, iWay, FusionWare, Sterling Commerce, SUN/SeeBeyond, TIBCO, webMethods. Good entrenchment; speed and cost of implementations challenged by SOA pioneers.
69% currently use or plan business process/workflow services within 12 months (survey, 286 companies).
58% implementing or plan governance/management of services within 12 months; Aberdeen recommends evaluating policy/governance management as ESB selection criterion.
